feedback.text: I am writing to ask that the Digital Fairness Act be expanded to specifically protect consumers from the destruction of digital video games that they have purchased. Hundreds of games have already been permanently disabled by publishers, cutting off access to titles that customers paid for, often with no compensation or alternative provided. This is especially concerning for online-only and live service games, which can be removed entirely at the discretion of companies, even if they are still popular or have active communities. I believe it is fundamentally unfair for a product that has been sold to consumers to be rendered permanently unusable simply because a publisher decides to discontinue support or shut down servers. This practice results in a loss of cultural heritage, wasted money for consumers, and erodes trust in digital markets. Other digital rights issues, such as dark patterns and gambling-like features in games, are already addressed in the Act, so it would be a natural extension to include protections against game destruction. Please consider adding clear requirements that publishers maintain access to games that have been sold, or require a proper end-of-life plan that ensures customers can continue to use what they paid foreither through patches, server emulation, or by otherwise providing the means to play offline. With over a million EU citizens supporting this issue through the Citizens Initiative, I hope you will recognize its urgency and importance. Thank you for your commitment to consumer rights.
